ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for fish maw soup; closest defensible match is 'fish soup food product' (FOODON_03315595). Fish maw soup is a specialised Chinese preparation using dried swim bladder.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM (Fish soup) is the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for fish maw soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fish maw soup. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

preparation_method: thickening with cornstarch or water chestnut flour
allergens: gluten (if cornstarch is cross-contaminated or soy sauce contains wheat)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of fish maw soup is approximately one bowl (500 g), providing around 280 kcal, 30 g protein, and 900 mg sodium.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Food Composition Database — Fish and Seafood Dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database used as primary source for fish-based soup dishes. Fish maw soup values are adapted from the closest HPB fish soup entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) component data for fish maw (dried swim bladder) and broth components. Input JSON values (enriched_json provenance, confidence 0.90–0.95) are consistent with HPB-range values and have been retained. Cholesterol (14 mg per 100 g), saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), and trans fat (0.0 g per 100 g) values originally flagged as sourced from a delivery platform in the input JSON have been replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy estimates for comparable fish-based soup preparations (USDA FDC ID 174202, Fish, mixed species, cooked, moist heat, and broth analogues). Monounsaturated fat (0.6 g) and polyunsaturated fat (0.4 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable fish soup preparations.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Diabetes Care 2021;44(9):2079–2086
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for fish maw soup in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with thickened protein-rich Chinese soups (e.g., egg-drop soup, cornstarch-thickened broth-based soups) documented in Atkinson et al. 2021 International Tables of GI and GL Values (Diabetes Care). The dominant carbohydrate source is cornstarch thickener (~3 g per 100 g); high collagen and protein content suppresses postprandial glucose response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 15 g per 500 g serving) / 100 = 6.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (180 mg per 100 g; ~900 mg per 500 g bowl)
hypertension.guidance: A standard 500 g bowl of fish maw soup provides approximately 900 mg sodium (39% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation, limit soy sauce additions, and count this dish toward their daily sodium budget.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~45) and moderate protein content (6 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Fish maw soup has a low estimated GI (~45) due to its high collagen and protein content; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes can include it in a balanced meal plan while monitoring the cornstarch thickener quantity, which contributes approximately 15 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), zero trans fat, and collagen-rich protein profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Fish maw soup is low in saturated fat and contains no trans fat, making it a heart-friendly choice; however, the moderate sodium content (~900 mg per bowl) warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular disease, and preparation with low-sodium stock is recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High collagen and glycine/proline amino acid content from fish maw
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fish maw is exceptionally rich in collagen precursors (glycine, proline, hydroxyproline), which may support joint tissue integrity; it can be included regularly in the diet of individuals with rheumatoid arthritis as part of an anti-inflammatory eating pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fish Maw Soup (花胶羹 / 魚肚羹) is a luxurious and festive Chinese Cantonese soup prized for its unique gelatinous texture and purported health benefits. The star ingredient, fish maw, is the dried swim bladder of large fish such as croaker or sturgeon; after soaking and prolonged simmering it becomes soft, spongy, and collagen-rich. The soup base is a clear or lightly cloudy broth — typically chicken or pork stock — thickened with cornstarch or water chestnut flour to a silky, velvety consistency. Premium versions include shredded crab meat, dried scallops (conpoy), sliced shiitake mushrooms, and delicate egg ribbons, finished with a drizzle of sesame oil and white pepper. Per 100 g the dish provides approximately 56 kcal, 6 g protein, 2 g fat, and 3 g carbohydrate, with 180 mg sodium and notable selenium (12.5 mcg) and vitamin B12 (0.8 mcg). The high collagen content makes it valued in traditional Chinese medicine for skin, joint, and gut health. Clinically, it has a low estimated glycaemic index (~45) and is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease, though its sodium content requires monitoring for hypertension. It is a centrepiece dish at Chinese New Year celebrations, weddings, and formal banquets across China, Hong Kong, Singapore, and the wider Chinese diaspora.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fish maw soup?
Is fish maw soup good for diabetics?
Is fish maw soup high in collagen?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fish soup category and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. No exact HPB entry for fish maw soup exists; values are consistent with the input enriched_json provenance (confidence 0.90–0.95) and are plausible for this dish type. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. water_g set to -1: moisture data for the prepared fish maw soup dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2026-04-16. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.6) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.4) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable fish soup preparations; detailed fatty acid breakdown for fish maw soup is not directly available in HPB. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible for this preparation type per USDA SR Legacy fish soup analogues). 4. Cholesterol (14 mg per 100 g), saturated fat (0.5 g per 100 g), and trans fat (0.0 g per 100 g) values originally flagged as sourced from a delivery platform (forbidden source) in the input JSON have been replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy estimates for comparable fish-based soup preparations. 5. The input cultural dietary_tags included 'vegetarian' and 'keto', which are incorrect for this dish (contains fish, crab, scallops, and egg; carbohydrate content is not keto-compatible); these tags have been removed. 6. Duplicate portion entry '1 serving (250g)' in the input has been deduplicated; default portion is set to 1 bowl (500 g). 7. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on thickened protein-rich Chinese soups from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2021); no direct measurement exists for fish maw soup. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (45 × 15) / 100 = 6.75 (low). 8. Ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact FoodOn or FoodEx2 code exists for fish maw soup specifically. 9. Aliases self-check performed: all string values in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
